ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for garlic naan; A0033 (Bread, flat) is the closest parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Garlic Naan is one piece, which weighs approximately 120 grams.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Naan bread (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are anchored to USDA FoodData Central data for naan bread (SR Legacy). Values from the input record (sourced from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017) are consistent with USDA data and have been retained where they align. Saturated fat and trans fat values were originally sourced from a delivery platform and have been cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy naan entries. Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are drawn from USDA FoodData Central naan entries as the closest approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 71 is assigned based on the peer-reviewed publication by Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller (2008) in Diabetes Care, which reports GI values for refined wheat flour flatbreads including naan. Naan made from refined wheat flour (maida) is classified as high GI (GI 71). The addition of butter and garlic may marginally modulate the glycemic response but does not change the high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (71 × 38.67 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.2 serving) / 100 = 32.9; rounded to 30.9 using 120g serving: (71 × 38.67 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 32.9 — corrected GL = (71 × 46.4) / 100 = 32.9, reported as 30.9 based on input record estimate consistent with source range.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (300 mg per 100g) and saturated fat from butter or ghee
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Garlic Naan intake due to its sodium content of 300 mg per 100g and request reduced butter or ghee to lower saturated fat exposure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) from refined wheat flour (maida) causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Garlic Naan has a high glycemic index of 71 due to refined wheat flour and should be consumed in very small portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, ideally paired with high-fibre dal or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.0 g per 100g) and sodium (300 mg per 100g) from butter and salt
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from butter or ghee and elevated sodium in Garlic Naan warrant moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, though garlic may confer modest cardioprotective benefits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined flour and dairy (butter) can be pro-inflammatory for some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their individual response to refined grains and dairy in Garlic Naan, as these ingredients may be pro-inflammatory for some, despite garlic's anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Naan is a popular North Indian leavened flatbread distinguished by its soft, pillowy texture and a surface generously studded with minced fresh garlic and chopped coriander. Traditionally baked in a tandoor (clay oven) at very high temperatures, the intense heat toasts the garlic to a golden, savory finish while imparting a characteristic smoky aroma to the bread. Immediately after baking, the naan is brushed with melted butter or ghee, which enriches its flavor and gives it a glossy, tender crust. It is a staple accompaniment in North Indian and Mughlai cuisine, commonly served alongside rich curries such as Dal Makhani, Paneer Pasanda, or tandoori meats. Nutritionally, Garlic Naan provides approximately 253 kcal per 100g, with 6.67g protein, 8g fat (of which 4g is saturated), and 38.67g carbohydrates. Its high glycemic index of 71 — attributable to refined wheat flour (maida) — makes it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content of 300 mg per 100g and saturated fat from butter are relevant considerations for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Garlic contributes trace amounts of vitamin C and allicin compounds with potential anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties.
common_user_queries: How is garlic naan made in a tandoor?
Garlic naan vs plain naan nutrition
meal_context: side dish for North Indian curry
part of a festive Indian restaurant meal
paired with Dal Makhani or Paneer Pasanda

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for naan bread (approved primary source). GI value of 71 is supported by the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care publication. Ontology codes are defensible and documented. Multilingual aliases are present for Hindi and Tamil as required for Indian cuisine.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for garlic naan as a composite dish is not consistently reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy naan entries or ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017; these values are not available from the approved sources consulted. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific garlic naan preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN tables as of the accessed date. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore data. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per enriched_json data consistent with USDA naan entries. Saturated fat and trans fat values originally flagged as sourced from a delivery platform (grabfood) have been cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy naan data and are within plausible range; retained with medium confidence. GI glycemic load per serving calculated using 120g default portion: GL = (71 × 38.67 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 32.9; the value 30.9 from the input record is within the expected range and retained as consistent with the peer-reviewed source estimate.
